questions.question: Define Object-Oriented Programming and explain its main advantage over procedural programming.
keyPoints: OOP is a programming paradigm that organizes code around objects containing data and behavior
Objects model real-world entities with identity, state, and behavior
Main advantage: Better code reusability through inheritance, modularity, maintainability, and natural modeling of real-world scenarios

questions.question: Explain the difference between a Class and an Object with an example.
keyPoints: Class is a blueprint/template defining attributes and methods; Object is an instance of a class
Class does not occupy memory; Object occupies memory when created
Example: Class 'Car' defines blueprint; objects 'myCar', 'yourCar' are actual instances with specific data

questions.question: List and briefly explain the four pillars of Object-Oriented Programming.
keyPoints: Encapsulation: Bundling data and methods into a single unit
Abstraction: Hiding complex details, showing only essential features
Inheritance: Deriving new classes from existing classes for code reuse
Polymorphism: Ability of objects to take many forms

questions.question: What is encapsulation? How is it achieved in OOP? Give one real-world example.
keyPoints: Encapsulation is bundling data and methods that operate on data into a single unit (class)
It restricts direct access to some components using access specifiers (private, protected)
Real-world example: Bank account - balance is private, transactions happen only through defined methods

questions.question: Write a brief history of Object-Oriented Programming mentioning at least three significant languages.
keyPoints: 1967: Simula developed by Ole-Johan Dahl and Kristen Nygaard - first OOP language with classes and inheritance
1970s: Smalltalk developed at Xerox PARC by Alan Kay - popularized pure OOP with dynamic typing
1980s: C++ developed by Bjarne Stroustrup - added OOP features to C
1995: Java developed by James Gosling - brought OOP to mainstream internet applications

questions.question: Differentiate between Abstraction and Encapsulation.
keyPoints: Abstraction hides implementation details, shows only essential features; Encapsulation bundles data and methods together
Abstraction focuses on 'what' an object does; Encapsulation focuses on 'how' it's done
Abstraction is achieved through abstract classes/interfaces; Encapsulation is achieved through access specifiers
Real-world: Abstraction = car dashboard (interface); Encapsulation = engine components bundled together

questions.question: Explain Inheritance and Polymorphism with suitable examples.
keyPoints: Inheritance: Mechanism where derived class inherits properties from base class (e.g., 'Dog' inherits from 'Animal')
Polymorphism: Same method behaves differently for different classes (e.g., 'sound()' method gives 'bark' for Dog, 'meow' for Cat)
Inheritance promotes code reuse; Polymorphism allows one interface for different data types

questions.question: Explain the fundamental concepts of Object-Oriented Programming. How does OOP differ from traditional procedural programming? Illustrate with examples.
keyPoints: Fundamentals: Objects (instances with state/behavior), Classes (blueprints), Encapsulation, Abstraction, Inheritance, Polymorphism
Difference from procedural: OOP groups data with functions that operate on it; procedural separates data and functions
OOP models real-world entities naturally; procedural uses functions and global data
Benefits: Reusability, maintainability, modularity, extensibility, data hiding

questions.question: What is a Class? What is the relationship between Class and Object? Explain with a C++ example showing how to define a class and create objects.
keyPoints: Class: User-defined data type that acts as blueprint for objects
Object: Instance of class, occupies memory, has state and behavior
Class defines attributes (data members) and methods (member functions)
C++ example: class Rectangle { private: int length, width; public: void setData(int l, int w){...} int area(){...} };
Main function: Rectangle r1, r2; r1.setData(5,3); - creates objects from class blueprint

questions.question: Describe the evolution of Object-Oriented Programming languages. Discuss the contribution of Simula and Smalltalk in the development of OOP.
keyPoints: Simula (1967): First OOP language by Dahl and Nygaard, introduced classes, inheritance, objects
Smalltalk (1970s): Pure OOP by Alan Kay at Xerox PARC, introduced message passing, dynamic typing, garbage collection
C++ (1980s): Hybrid language by Stroustrup, added OOP to C with classes, inheritance, polymorphism
Java (1995): Platform-independent OOP by Gosling, popular for internet applications
Python, C#, Ruby followed, bringing OOP to modern programming

questions.question: Explain the benefits of Object-Oriented Programming over procedural programming. Why is OOP preferred for large software development?
keyPoints: Code Reusability: Inheritance allows reusing code from parent classes
Modularity: Each object is self-contained, easy to manage
Maintainability: Changes in one part don't affect others due to encapsulation
Data Hiding: Encapsulation protects data from unauthorized access
Real-world Modeling: OOP naturally represents real-world entities
Scalability: OOP makes it easier to extend and modify software
Team Development: Different teams can work on different classes independently
